content:
# 1 John Chapter 4 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/1JN_003_page_001.jpg

1. Beloved, do not believe every spirit, but test the spirits to see whether they are from God, for many false prophets have gone out into the world.

2. By this you know the Spirit of God: every spirit that confesses that Jesus Christ has come in the flesh is from God,

3. and every spirit that does not confess Jesus is not from God. This is the spirit of the antichrist, which you heard was coming and now is in the world already.

4. Little children, you are from God and have overcome them, for he who is in you is greater than he who is in the world.

5. They are from the world; therefore they speak from the world, and the world listens to them.

6. We are from God. Whoever knows God listens to us; whoever is not from God does not listen to us. By this we know the Spirit of truth and the spirit of error.

7. Beloved, let us love one another, for love is from God, and whoever loves has been born of God and knows God.

8. Anyone who does not love does not know God, because God is love.

9. In this the love of God was made manifest among us, that God sent his only Son into the world, so that we might live through him.

10. In this is love, not that we have loved God but that he loved us and sent his Son to be the propitiation for our sins.

11. Beloved, if God so loved us, we also ought to love one another.

12. No one has ever seen God; if we love one another, God abides in us and his love is perfected in us.

13. By this we know that we abide in him and he in us, because he has given us of his Spirit.

14. And we have seen and testify that the Father has sent his Son to be the Savior of the world.

15. Whoever confesses that Jesus is the Son of God, God abides in him, and he in God.

16. So we have come to know and to believe the love that God has for us. God is love, and whoever abides in love abides in God, and God abides in him.

17. By this is love perfected with us, so that we may have confidence for the day of judgment, because as he is so also are we in this world.

18. There is no fear in love, but perfect love casts out fear. For fear has to do with punishment, and whoever fears has not been perfected in love.

19. We love because he first loved us.

20. If anyone says, "I love God," and hates his brother, he is a liar; for he who does not love his brother whom he has seen cannot love God whom he has not seen.

21. And this commandment we have from him: whoever loves God must also love his brother.

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

Chapter 4 is the theological and emotional center of 1 John. The twin declarations "God is love" (vv.8, 16) are the letter's summit. The chapter moves from christological testing (vv.1-6) through love's origin in God (vv.7-12) to love's perfection in us (vv.13-21). The logic is circular but not repetitive — each cycle deepens: God's nature → God's act → our response → God's indwelling → our confidence.

**Verse 10 — "Not that we loved God but that he loved us":** The chapter's hinge. All human love is responsive, never initiatory. This reversal distinguishes Christian love from every other ethical system.

---
## ⚠️ Disagreement Log

### v.9 — "his only Son" vs. "his one and only Son"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "his only-begotten Son" | ton huion autou ton monogenē |
| LOGOS | "his only Son" | monogenēs = only, unique |
| PNEUMA | "his one and only Son" | Emphasis on uniqueness |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. monogenēs means "only" or "unique" — the term emphasizes singularity, not the mode of generation. EMET's "only-begotten" imports the later theological debate about gennēsis into a word that simply means "one of a kind." PNEUMA's "one and only" is redundant — "only" already means "one and only." LOGOS's "only Son" is precise and sufficient.

**Status:** Minor. Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

### v.10 — "propitiation" vs. "sacrifice to take away our sins"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "propitiation for our sins" | hilasmon peri tōn hamartiōn hēmōn |
| LOGOS | "the propitiation for our sins" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "a sacrifice to take away our sins" | Interprets hilasmos as expiation only |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. Same issue as 2:2 — hilasmos carries both propitiation (averting wrath) and expiation (removing sin). PNEUMA's "sacrifice to take away our sins" captures expiation but loses propitiation. Consistency with 2:2 requires the same rendering.

**Status:** ⚠️ Theologically significant. Consistent with 2:2 ruling. Propitiation vs. expiation remains the key issue.

---

### v.17 — "as he is so also are we in this world" vs. "we live like Jesus here in this world"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "just as that [one] is, so also are we in this world" | kathōs ekeinos estin kai hēmeis esmen en tō kosmō toutō |
| LOGOS | "as he is so also are we in this world" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "we live like Jesus here in this world" | "Live like Jesus" for "as he is, so are we" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The Greek says "as that one is (estin), so also are we (esmen)" — a statement of ontological likeness, not behavioral imitation. PNEUMA's "live like Jesus" makes it ethical ("imitate his lifestyle"), but the author's point is identity: we are in this world as he is — loved by the Father, confident in judgment, already sharing his nature. The present tenses are key: "as he is... so are we."

**Status:** ⚠️ Theologically significant. Ontological identity vs. ethical imitation.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Description |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA changes propitiation | 1 | "Sacrifice to take away sins" for hilasmos (v.10) |
| PNEUMA converts ontology to ethics | 1 | "Live like Jesus" for "as he is, so are we" (v.17) |
| PNEUMA adds emphasis | 1 | "One and only" for "only" (v.9) |

**Overall pattern:** Chapter 4's most significant disagreement is v.17 — whether our relationship to Christ in this world is ontological (we are as he is) or ethical (we live like him). The author's point is deeper than imitation: believers share Christ's standing before the Father, which is why they can have confidence on judgment day. The propitiation issue (v.10) continues the pattern from 2:2.

---

*— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA*
